data.title: Alphabet: Five Reasons To Buy The Shares
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate the recommendation to buy Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) shares. This article is the continuation of my coverage initiation thesis published on December 12, 2024. It is also important to highlight that the company is part of my recommended stock portfolio for 2025. The company's shares are one of the highlights of the year so far, and in this article, my intention is to give five strong arguments for the investor to buy the company's shares, and suppose what Berkshire would have done invest in the company.

Context

In the first week of the year I wrote an exclusive article for Seeking Alpha offering investors my recommended portfolio of stocks, and since then, the performance has been a success. Year to date the portfolio rises 24.5% while the SPY ETF rises 14.6%.

One of the holdings in the portfolio was the shares of Alphabet , due to its exposure to AI, quality of management and mainly, the discount compared to the others magnificent seven. Since then, the shares have risen 46%, and I'll show you why I remain confident in the investment thesis.

First Argument

I don't know if you realized this, but when we talk about artificial intelligence, Alphabet is the only large company that is vertically integrated. We can see below that we have companies working on applications, language models, cloud, and hardware. But only the owner of Google operates in all segments.

Becoming vertically integrated presents challenges, without a doubt, however, management has already demonstrated its ability to operate different businesses. Additionally, verticalization will bring gains in efficiency, synergy, and bring resilience to the operation, which I consider the first major reason for investors to have Alphabet in their portfolio.

Second Argument

The second argument has already been mentioned in previous articles, and is related to OpenAI and ChatGPT . Many analysts suggested that ChatGPT would kill Google's search engine. From the beginning I disagreed with this thesis, however, now I can refute it with authority through the graph below.

Data shows that the search engine still has more than 5 trillion searches per year, and revenue continues to grow despite the launch of ChatGPT. Although ChatGPT has changed the market balance, the second argument for investors to have Alphabet shares in their portfolio is its moat in the search engine.

Third Argument

Every week I publish an article about the American economy where I argue that we are not in an AI bubble, but even if we were in a scenario minimally similar to a bubble, Alphabet would be one of the best options to have in the portfolio.

As we can see, the company has one of the most robust cash reserves with $98 billion, behind only Microsoft and Berkshire Hathway , which would already be a great argument for investors to include Alphabet shares in their portfolio.

The most curious thing is that many analysts use Berkshire Hathaway's cash as one of the arguments to say that we are in a bubble, and close to a market crash. However, this argument seems to have gone down the drain with recent news. Despite rumors of an AI bubble, Berkshire Hathaway announced on November 14 that it acquired 17.8 million shares of Alphabet , in an investment of $4.3 billion.

Fourth Argument

Despite the recent appreciation of shares, the fourth argument is valuation. I argue that company shares will have multiples repriced due to the productivity gains that AI will bring, and also due to the interest rate cuts process. Therefore, my target for the company's P/E is an ATH of 33.5x earnings.

In this scenario, the company's shares trade at 27x projected earnings, compared to the target of 33.5x earnings, we have an upside of 24%, which corroborates my recommendation to buy the shares. Given my argument about valuation, I feel confident with the recommendation to buy when I see the grades of growth,profitability, momentum and revisions.

Another incredible analysis is how the company manages to beat increasingly optimistic market estimates. Since the first quarter of 2023, the company has beaten market estimates, with a single exception in revenues for the fourth quarter of 2024.

The company surprised the market in its latest results with robust growth in the search adverstising , Youtube , and in the promising cloud segment. Honestly, after listing all these arguments, it is difficult not to be optimistic about the investment thesis, in any case, let's know the risks to the thesis.

Fifth Argument

In my opinion, the most powerful argument, which should have motivated Berkshire Hathway to invest in the company, is exposure to the quantum computing segment. Overall, McKinsey projects that the quantum technology market could reach $198 billion by 2040.

It is a technology to solve problems that are unsolvable today, such as creating more efficient batteries, modeling financial risks with thousands of assumptions and variables, simulating molecules, accelerating the discovery of new medicines and the advent of AI.

Recently the company announced the Willow chip and the Quantum Echoes algorithm. Both positioned Alphabet very well in a market with great competition from technology companies and even banks.

Therefore, in my opinion Alphabet is extremely valuable for all the businesses it already has, but this embryonic quantum computing business could be one of the biggest markets of the future.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

We have known risks that have already been addressed in other articles, such as antitrust issues, regulatory challenges, competition in the search engine market, retention of talent in AI.

Not to mention that shares have already risen more than 45% in the year, meaning the risk-return ratio is no longer as attractive. But in this article I want to delve deeper into a risk that may be new to many investors. As we know, companies like Alphabet, Amazon , Meta and others are making strong investments in AI.

It recently caught my attention that Meta moved $30 billion in debt related to artificial intelligence off its financial balance sheet through special purpose vehicles ( SPVs ) , many analysts saw this as a financial engineering maneuver reminiscent of the 2008 crisis. In a simplified way, through this strategy Meta achieves increase debt and maintain stable leverage.

But of course, we are talking about Alphabet, not Meta. Well, recently Google came in as a guarantor for a data center expansion project by the bitcoin mining company TeraWulf worth $3 billion. We know that a lot of investments will be necessary, but it seems to me to be a strategy very similar to Meta, and I will analyze the company's results with even more caution to identify businesses of this type.

The Bottom Line

I started writing articles for Seeking Alpha in 2024, and with the aim of delivering even more value to the reader, I listed the stocks that would be part of my recommended stock portfolio for 2025. Alphabet shares were included in this portfolio. The year is coming to an end, and the company's shares are the main highlight of portfolio.

The verticalized AI business model provides resilience to the operation, and in parallel, ChatGPT was unable to displace Google's search engine. Additionally, the company has robust cash flow if it goes through adverse times, and the earnings momentum is very strong.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy the company's shares. In my opinion, the risk-return ratio remains very attractive. In my calculations, we still have a robust upside of 24% to invest in the company's shares.

data.title: Amazon: OpenAI Deal Is A Game Changer
data.description:
Business is booming for Amazon (AMZN). The company’s third-quarter earnings on October 30th painted a clear picture of strength across the board, led once again by its crown jewel, Amazon Web Services (AWS). The results were met with enthusiasm from investors, pushing the stock to new all-time highs.

In Q325, Amazon crushed it in cloud, with the business growing more quickly than in the prior quarter, proving investors wrong that cast doubt on the company’s AWS growth. The use of artificial intelligence is starting to have an enterprise-wide effect on the company as Amazon is growing more quickly, which in turn is driving significant earnings growth. Shares hit a record high following Q325 earnings, yet my view on Amazon has never been more bullish.

Growth and Margin Upside for Years to Come

Amazon’s Q3 2025 results came in ahead of expectations, fueled by steady retail sales and a meaningful reacceleration in AWS. Every segment, from domestic and international e-commerce to cloud services, delivered at least 11% year-over-year growth. The U.S. retail business alone generated more than $106 billion in turnover during the quarter, continuing to anchor Amazon’s massive top line.

The real standout, though, was AWS. Segment revenue climbed 20% year-over-year to $33.0 billion, up from 17.5% growth in the prior quarter. That improvement might sound small, but it’s a significant swing given AWS’s scale, and it reaffirms that demand for Amazon’s AI and cloud offerings remains strong. Total company revenue grew 13% year-over-year, proving that AWS continues to act as Amazon’s main growth engine.

After years of prioritizing reinvestment, Amazon is finally hitting its stride in profitability. Third-quarter net income reached $21.2 billion, or roughly $230 million in profit per day. On an annualized basis, that’s about $84 billion in earnings power over the next twelve months, potentially more if AWS keeps accelerating.

No analysis of Amazon would be complete without shining a light on the AWS segment, which is the primary growth driver for the company. In the third quarter, AWS earned $11.4 billion from selling services on its cloud platform, mostly to corporations like SAP (SAP), ServiceNow (NOW), or Qantas Airways (OTCPK:QABSY). Amazon’s cloud platform is still the most sought after in the market, which should translate to years of sustainable net revenue growth. Per App Economy Insights, AWS has a significant lead in the cloud infrastructure market with a 29% market take-rate, leading Microsoft (MSFT) by 9 percentage points.

Profitability Momentum in the Cloud

AI-driven demand, both from enterprise and consumer clients, continues to fuel AWS’s profitability. One of the best metrics to gauge this is operating income margin (OIM), calculated as operating income divided by net sales. In Q3, AWS’s OIM rose to 34.5%, up from 32.9% the previous quarter.

That margin expansion tells the story: AWS is not only growing revenue but also operating more efficiently. Amazon’s total operating income climbed 10% year-over-year to $11.4 billion, showing how the cloud segment’s performance is lifting the company overall.

As growth catalysts for Amazon, I would point to the company’s AWS acceleration momentum, higher operating income, a rebound in operating income margins in Q325, and the company’s long-term potential in the CSP market, catalyzed by higher capex in the industry as a whole.

AWS/OpenAI Deal Is Set To Accelerate Amazon’s Cloud Growth

AWS announced a $38 billion deal for cloud services with OpenAI earlier this month. This deal, which will see its contract value get spread out over a seven-year period, will give OpenAI access to Amazon’s GB200 and GB300 chips that are deployed in the company’s Amazon EC2 UltraServers. OpenAI is taking a major step forward here in the diversification of its revenue profile, as the company has previously been reliant on Microsoft for its cloud infrastructure. But the deal is obviously much more significant for Amazon.

First, the deal sets the stage for a revenue acceleration for AWS. The cloud services agreement will see a ramp-up period until the end of 2026, which is when Amazon will be in a stage of full-capacity deployment. This means that AWS could grow even faster than it is now and become a LOT more profitable in the future.

The OpenAI contract, worth an average of $5.4 billion per year, will not only accelerate AWS growth but also hand the company a strategic win over its peers, particularly Azure and Google Cloud Platform. In 2024, AWS generated $39.8 billion in operating income, which I see developing to more than $186 billion by 2029, using growth rates of 35% (2026) and then using a sliding percentage schedule.

Over the horizon of my projection period (2025-2029), Amazon’s growth could yield a more than 3.2x increase in net revenue and a 4.7x increase in operating income. I expect to see a significant increase in operating margins over time as AWS benefits as a leading CSP from growing cloud compute and chip demand. This should fundamentally benefit the company’s margins as it expands its international data center footprint. I am particularly optimistic about AWS given the growing size of the server market, which underpins a bullish sector outlook more broadly. The projections below are based on the idea that Amazon will be able to grow its net revenue at a quicker pace in the future than in the past, with the OpenAI contract providing a catalyst.

What’s more, AWS is already profitable to a serious extent, which makes cloud platform-derived growth so much more valuable than revenue growth in e-commerce. In my calculation below, I highlight the estimated trajectory of AWS revenue, though I plan with a decelerating growth rate after 2027 (to be conservative). As such, my net revenue growth rate in the AWS segment will fall slowly over time to about 20% in 2029. The most important insight here is that a higher revenue base in the cloud segment and growing operating income margins are set to ignite an explosion in operating income growth for AWS, which may in the future even lead to a major share buyback.

It is entirely within the realms of possibility, in my estimation, for Amazon’s cloud platform to grow to a near $200 billion income base at the end of my five-year planning horizon in 2029. To achieve this, Amazon can sign new (high-margin) AWS partnership deals with major corporations, expand geographically to high-growth regions such as the Asian-Pacific, and sell more custom-designed chips (like Trainium and Inferentia), which are optimized for AI/ML workloads.

Amazon’s Trainium2 chip revenue rose 150% YoY growth in Q325, with the business of producing custom-designed chips offering a long-term growth runway for AWS, thanks to growing confidence in capex spending. Trainium chip sales in particular could be a source of cloud strength for AWS in the future and drive interest in the company’s shares.

Valuation: Amazon’s Cloud Potential Isn’t Fully Priced In

Amazon reached a record high after Q325 as investors cheered the company’s business performance, and its cloud performance led to particular praise. With Q425 likely also shaping up to be a good quarter, at least for the e-commerce part of the company’s business, I see upside for Amazon’s shares.

Amazon’s shares go for 31.4x FWD earnings vs. 25.1x for Alphabet (GOOG) and 30.9x for Microsoft, all of which compete in the same cloud market. I used consensus estimates from SA to derive FWD P/S and P/E ratios for the three CSPs below. Microsoft seems to be particularly expensive based on net revenue (P/S of 11.3x), while Amazon is actually the CSP with the lowest net revenue multiple (P/S of 3.3x). The market clearly assigns a premium to Microsoft’s Azure growth story, but I’d argue Amazon deserves similar treatment. AWS has nearly identical growth potential, if not greater, given its scale and leading market share. If Amazon’s valuation were to move closer to Alphabet’s P/S of 7.4x, the upside could be substantial.

With an estimated 2026 revenue of $792 billion, Amazon remains the largest cloud and e-commerce player in the world. Based on a fair forward P/S multiple of 4.0x to 5.0x, I arrive at an equity fair value range between $3.2 trillion and $4.0 trillion, or roughly $296 to $370 per share. Compared to the current share price of $244, that suggests potential upside of 21% to 52%. Amazon’s share price as of the time of writing this article is $222, so shares trade a cool 25% below my low-end fair value. The discount, as far as I can tell, is due to the market underestimating the scaling of the cloud footprint as well as the magnitude of the operating earnings ramp that is likely to transpire in the next five years.

Moreover, the large market share of approximately 30% is a major asset for Amazon, and the OpenAI backing creates a strong lever for accelerating net revenue growth.

Risks to Watch

Large and small U.S. businesses are spending big bucks on AI to evolve and enhance their value propositions for their own customers. This growth comes primarily from the conviction that AI will deliver productivity benefits and lower costs for corporations, leading to higher returns on invested capital.

Recently, however, concerns have mounted in the market about a potential AI bubble, which led to dropping market valuations last week, particularly in the ‘Magnificent 7’ group, after Big Short investor Michael Burry disclosed a short position in Palantir Technologies (PLTR) and NVIDIA (NVDA).

If AI bubble concerns prove to be accurate, the market may be in for a rough ride and a loss of confidence in CSP’s net revenue growth potential.

My Verdict

In summary, Amazon’s growth is not showing any signs of slowing down, and capital expenditures are expected to rise drastically going forward, which kind of bodes well for AWS, Amazon’s locomotive for growth. In the third quarter, the AWS segment grew to $33.0 billion in net revenue, which makes AWS easily a $130 billion annual business, and its growth is picking up steam.

With AI and cloud services growing, AWS is in a robust position to leverage its cloud services platform, a platform that is still leading the CSP market by a decent amount. The AWS/OpenAI deal is a game changer, in my opinion, and points to a growth acceleration for Amazon. Serious margin and operating income gains await investors.

With strong earnings momentum, a clear lead in cloud infrastructure, and attractive valuation multiples, Amazon remains my top growth pick for the long term.

data.title: PFN: Has Done Well This Year, Double-Digit Yield
data.description:
Thesis

The PIMCO Income Strategy Fund II (PFN) is a closed end fund that we own and last covered in the beginning of the year. In that piece we upgraded the fund and outlined its conservative stance in a tough environment for credit.

In today's article we are going to revisit the CEF and provide an analysis of its performance in 2025, as well as outline its forward given the current macro forces.

A robust 2025

Fixed income closed end funds are supposed to be leveraged approaches for traditional high yield. This means they tend to outperform both on the upside and on the downside. Versus a staple like the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK), PFN has done well throughout the years:

We are big believers in visual representations, and the above table is quite good at highlighting the performances for the two names. As we can see from the above, PFN has historically been 1.5x the return for JNK, with a couple of outliers (2024 was a very good year for PFN against JNK, while 2020 was not).

PFN currently sports a 15% leverage ratio and also comes with an active management approach. This means that PFN can pivot towards corners of the fixed income market where undervaluation exists, while JNK sticks to its passive mandate. Let us see the current CEF composition.

Current fund composition

By looking on the CEF's webpage we can see its current asset allocation:

  • High yield credit: 39.5%
  • Non Usd developed: 16%
  • EM: 13.2%
  • Non Agency Mortgage: 10%
  • US Government Related: 9.5%
  • Invest. Grade Credit: 6.5%
  • CMBS: 3%
  • Other: residual

From a pure composition perspective the CEF is overweight U.S high yield, followed by developed markets bonds and EM debt. The portfolio duration is 3.5 years, and the CEF gives investors a 'duration weighted exposure' column, basically outlining where duration lies within the portfolio. For example the US Government related bucket has a 38% duration weighted fund exposure. This simply means the CEF holds treasuries that have a longer duration than the 3.5 years for the portfolio. Conversely the HY credit bucket is just 15% of the portfolio duration weighted, telling us the credits held there have a very short duration profile.

This set-up is good, because it limits the downside moves when credit spreads will blow higher. Furthermore if treasuries rally during a credit crisis the fund is set to benefit. There is therefore an embedded hedge in the portfolio here.

The fund does not disclose its ratings matrix, but given the asset buckets above we can guesstimate that only a very small proportion of the CEF is rated investment grade (namely the IG credit bucket, the treasuries bucket and potentially non agency mortgages). Our rough estimate is for a 25% to 30% allocation to IG credits here currently.

Volatility metrics and other analytics

Being a CEF with leverage, the fund is more volatile than a simple ETF like JNK. Morningstar gives us a 6% standard deviation (3-year) and a 1.39 Sharpe ratio. Let us see some other analytics here:

  • AUM: $0.7 bil
  • Distribution: 11.7%
  • Premium to NAV: 2.6%
  • z-stat: -1.4

We recently saw a move down in premiums on all of the Pimco CEFs, with this fund affected as well:

The CEF is trading handily within its historic 0% to 8% range, thus making the name fairly valued in our opinion when using this valuation metric.

Distribution is not covered

Given the low leverage ratio and tight spreads in the market, one could have guessed that the 11.7% distribution is not entirely covered:

As of a recent Section 19a notice, we can observe a 23% utilization of 'paid-in surplus' or return of capital. So the real cash flow backed distribution is closer to 9%, which is much more in-line with the total return which the fund is set to make this year.

From our view-point we understand the marketing aspect of having a high distribution, and are fine with ROC figures below 30%. We appreciate the fact that the fund has moved down the leverage spectrum and would rather see a lower distribution than a high leverage ratio.

Our only issue with the CEF is a macro one - tight spreads

At this juncture of the macro cycle, we view high-yield corporate credit as significantly overextended:

If we look at the ICE BofA Single-B US High Yield Index Option Adjusted Spreads, we can see they are at the bottom of the 10-year historic range. This simply means the next move is going to be up. We do not know when, but we would not want to buy HY here. Wait for a widening in spreads.

As we saw from our historic analysis, PFN will usually give a 1.5x move in respect to JNK. If JNK goes down by -10%, expect a -15% move in PFN. Thus a blow out in spreads will see a significant drawdown here. As a reference point, the fund had a -9% drawdown earlier in April 2025 when the market switched to a risk-off mode.

Conclusion

PFN is a fixed income closed end fund from Pimco. The CEF usually provides for a 1.5x return versus JNK, and has done very well in 2025, being close to a +10% total return for the year. The name has reduced its leverage this year, but will not escape a risk-off environment. We find the name fit for purpose, with no unnecessary risk factors. We like what the CEF is doing and will continue to hold, although we are most certainly concerned about tight credit spreads.

data.title: Nvidia: Bears Got This One Chart All Wrong (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) truly delivered a stellar quarter, showing a reacceleration in the Data Center segment, and setting an overall very bullish tone in the earnings call.

The stock is rightly up after these earnings, but bears are still trying their best to defend their position.

The latest “red flag” is the inventory growth, which some are labelling as channel stuffing.

However, upon further inspection, it seems to me like inventory isn’t out of proportion given sales growth and revenues.

This is not a Cisco (CSCO) of 1999 or an overvalued dot-com era company.

Having said this, investors should be wary about the true limitations and bottlenecks that Nvidia faces.

I am upgrading to a Buy rating.

Nvidia Q3

In my last post, I highlighted three risk factors going into Q3 earnings:

  • China.

  • AI bubble.

  • Competition.

At least on two of these points, I am now more confident after the Q3 earnings.

First off, Data Center revenue accelerated QoQ, up over 24%.

This is very encouraging, and paired with some of the comments from the earnings call, has eased my concerns of an AI bubble.

Salesforce's engineering team has seen at least 30% productivity increase in new co-development after adopting Cursor.

Source: Earnings Call.

And that’s not the only example that was given. AI is having tangible effects in businesses, something which I also highlighted in my last Palantir (PLTR) article.

Blackwell sales are off the charts, and cloud GPUs are sold out… we’ve entered the virtuous cycle of AI.

Source: Earnings Call.

It certainly doesn’t feel in the numbers like AI is slowing down. And even though I still believe competitors like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) are putting up a good fight, there’s still a very healthy demand for Nvidia’s GPUs.

What I was right about, of course, was the issues with China.

H20 sales were approximately $50 million, sizable purchase orders never materialized in the quarter due to geopolitical issues and the increasingly competitive market in China.

Source: Earnings Call.

Basically negligible revenues in China, and the company is still not including any potential China revenues in their forward guidance.

And in terms of the margins, it does seem like Nvidia’s margins are near their upper bound, and it might be difficult to squeeze further out.

But overall, a great quarter which supports the bullish case.

The “Red Flag”

Nonetheless, there was one, on the surface, interesting point that bears made after this report.

Nvidia inventories have climbed through the roof over the last few quarters.

If demand is so high, why is there so much inventory?

Indeed, this could be a way of making revenues look better, but I don’t see that as the case at all.

If anything, high inventory can be explained by the fact that there is a huge expected demand.

Management has already guided towards $0.5 trillion in Blackwell and Rubin revenue.

This is massive.

On the other hand, consider the fact that inventory relative to revenues sits near very normal levels.

You’d expect inventory to grow as fast as revenues and sales. There’s nothing strange about this in my opinion, and this has been the way Nvidia has operated in the past, too.

In short, the "high" inventory isn't a sign of weakness; it's a buffer against even higher anticipated demand, helping Nvidia maintain its near-monopoly in AI chips while minimizing stockouts that could cede market share to rivals like AMD or custom silicon from cloud giants.

Other Considerations

This doesn’t mean there aren’t reasons for concern in the future.

The lack of China revenues is still an issue, and what’s more concerning is the geopolitical tension, which could hamper Nvidia’s supply capacity.

But perhaps the most glaring risk is that the AI rollout could soon run into an energy bottleneck.

Or rather, the current power grid could prove insufficient to accommodate all the new data center buildout.

This is certainly something investors should watch out for.

Final Thoughts

All in all, this was an outstanding quarter for Nvidia. It proved that it remains the king of GPUs and that concerns of an AI bubble are overplayed, at least for now.

Nvidia is still a solid Buy. I did say I would buy a dip after earnings. Well, we did get a dip, though it was before earnings, and now it seems like Nvidia will soon be making new highs.

data.title: Hartford Equity Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

US equities surged in the third quarter, registering a sizable return year-to-date. Technology and small-cap stocks realized significant gains, and growth stocks outpaced their value counterparts by a wide margin. Lower trade-policy uncertainty, robust economic growth, and stronger-than-anticipated second-quarter corporate earnings fueled the equity rally despite concerns about inflation, tariffs, and a cooling labor market. GDP grew at a 3.8% annualized pace in the second quarter, thanks to robust consumer spending, and the Atlanta Federal Reserve Bank’s GDP tracker estimates that GDP will expand 3.8% in the third quarter, markedly higher than a previous estimate of 3.3%. According to FactSet, second-quarter earnings for companies in the S&P 500 Index grew by a stout 12.0% year over year, markedly better than the 4.80% forecast on June 30. Earnings are projected to grow 7.90% in the third quarter.

Performance Summary

Hartford Equity Income Fund (I Share) underperformed the Russell 1000 Value Index during the quarter.

Sector allocation, a result of our bottom-up stock-selection process, was the primary driver of relative underperformance. Allocation effect was driven by our underweight to communication services, overweight to consumer staples, and underweight to consumer discretionary but partially offset by our overweight to utilities.

Stock selection also detracted from relative performance. Weak selection in energy, consumer staples, and real estate was partially offset by strong selection in materials, healthcare, and utilities.

At the issuer level, our top-two relative detractors were not holding benchmark constituent Alphabet (communication services) and an overweight to Keurig Dr Pepper (consumer staples), while our top-two relative contributors were out-of-benchmark positions in Barrick Mining (materials) and TE Connectivity (information technology).

Positioning & Outlook

As we look toward the end of the year, the large-cap universe is rich with new opportunities. We continue to be laser-focused on finding high-quality businesses with attractive valuation and capital returns. We take a balanced approach to portfolio construction—not leaning too heavily on any one scenario—and we always strive to upgrade the quality of the portfolio.

At the end of the period, our largest sector overweights were to healthcare, utilities, and real estate. We were most underweight to communication services, financials, and consumer discretionary.

The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For more current performance information to the most recent month ended, please visit hartfordfunds.com.

Performance shown prior to the inception of a class reflects performance and operating expenses of another class(es) (excluding sales charges, if applicable). Had fees and expenses of a class been reflected for the periods prior to the inception of that class, performance would be different. Since inception (SI) performance is from 8/28/03. Performance and expenses for other share classes will vary. Additional information is in the prospectus. Only Class A assesses a sales charge. Performance for periods of less than one year is not annualized.

Benchmark: Russell 1000 Value Index is an unmanaged index measuring the performance of those Russell 1000 Index companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. Indices are unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

1Expenses are the total annual operating expenses from the Fund's most recent prospectus at the time of publication.

Important Risks: Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Security prices fluctuate in value depending on general market and economic conditions and the prospects of individual companies. • For dividend-paying stocks, dividends are not guaranteed and may decrease without notice. • The securities of large market capitalization companies may underperform other segments of the market. • Mid-cap securities can have greater risks and volatility than large-cap securities. • Value investing style may go in and out of favor, which may cause the Fund to underperform other equity funds using different investment styles. • To the extent the Fund focuses on one or more sectors, the Fund may be subject to increased volatility and risk of loss if adverse developments occur. • Foreign investments may be more volatile and less liquid than U.S. investments and are subject to the risk of currency fluctuations and adverse political, economic and regulatory developments.

Investors should carefully consider a fund's investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. This and other important information is contained in a fund's full prospectus and summary prospectus, which can be obtained by visiting hartfordfunds.com. Please read it carefully before investing.

Mutual funds are distributed by Hartford Funds Distributors, LLC (HFD), Member FINRA. Advisory services are provided by Hartford Funds Management Company, LLC (HFMC). Certain funds are sub-advised by Wellington Management Company LLP. HFMC and Wellington Management are SEC registered investment advisers. HFD and HFMC are not affiliated with any sub-adviser.

NOT FDIC INSURED • MAY LOSE VALUE • NO BANK GUARANTEE

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nvidia Q3: The Flipside Of 'Everything Is Sold Out' Is Concerning
data.description:
How to Define AI Bubble?

Recently, we saw some market pullback driven by fears of an AI bubble and delayed Fed rate cuts. Some argued that we are already in an AI bubble and it's time to sell AI stocks, including the sector's "crown jewel," Nvidia Corporation (NVDA). Others believe that the current AI valuations are reasonable and still have more room to expand. NVDA's robust 3Q FY2026 earnings reignited risk-on sentiment in the market. Keep in mind, a strong result from NVDA can't tell if AI spending is overdone or not, because higher AI spending simply boosted the company's revenue growth.

Now, how do we define an AI bubble? It could mean stretched valuation multiples, AI overspending, and a big gap between upfront AI capex and profitability. I believe bubbles or lofty valuations tend to unwind sharply during periods of distressed market conditions, which the market is currently downplaying.

NVDA is a good example. Its growth and margin profile is "best in class" and showed sequential improvement in Q3 FY2026. From Blackwell to the next-gen Rubin platform (with rack-scale integration). CEO Jensen Huang in the press release noted, "Blackwell sales are off the charts, and cloud GPUs are sold out."

However, in my last analysis, I maintained a Hold on NVDA not because of its 40x forward P/E, but due to a risk of slowdown in 2026. Some might argue that NVDA is nowhere near any slowdown, especially after Q3 earnings. While I acknowledge that NVDA's recent earnings suggest an accelerating growth momentum, AI spending outlook in 2026 has been revised sharply higher since the beginning of this year, without considering any macro risk.

NVDA generates robust growth as long as hyperscalers aggressively expand AI infrastructure. The expansion is basically about front-loading GPUs based on expectations that these AI applications and services will generate a big payoff in the long run. However, the payoff from these AI investments will take time to materialize. I also think that the current AI spending outlook for 2026 has priced in perfection, which increases the risk of a near-term demand shock. For these reasons, adding more positions at the current price levels is premature.

Robust AI Spending Has Been Priced In

Before we get into the NVDA's Q3 earnings, I want to recap the current AI spending outlook. According to Bloomberg, Gartner now expects global AI spending to grow 40% to reach $2 trillion in 2026. The strong growth reflects a number of mega deals from hyperscalers and the expansion of OpenAI partnerships with major GPU suppliers, including NVDA. The company is expected to deploy at least 10 GW for OpenAI. This basically tells us why the AI stocks experienced strong rallies and pushed their valuations even higher.

Gartner also projects total AI spending to reach $3.3 trillion by 2029. Jensen Huang also estimates $3 to $4 trillion in AI capex by the end of this decade. If AI capex consensus continues breaking new records, it's not surprising to see AI-driven rallies again in the market. NVDA would be the biggest beneficiary.

But this brings us to a key question: if a 40% growth in AI capex next year has already been priced in, what happens if it turns out to be below the current expectations? If the market has predicted the AI spending outlook poorly in the past, it also means we could get hit with a negative surprise. If NVDA's valuation is based on the current consensus, then a 40x forward P/E may appear reasonable. But if consensus turns out to be too high, NVDA's forward multiple could jump higher and make the price look more expensive than we previously assumed. That's why I believe although NVDA's valuation is not stretched, the risk is still there and is moving higher.

Q3 FY2026 Is Strong, Supported by Accelerating AI Spending Outlook

The 2026 AI spending outlook supports NVDA's Datacenter revenue. Despite a $4 billion headwind for H20, the Datacenter still achieved an incredible 25% QoQ growth in Q3 FY2026. The company has not shipped any H2O to China yet. Management is projecting zero H20 revenue from China for the foreseeable future, which will be reflected in their forward guidance.

The company's total revenue grew 62.5% YoY in Q3, accelerating from 55.6% YoY in Q2. Moreover, it also guided 4Q total revenue of $65 billion (plus or minus 2%), which easily beat consensus. At the midpoint, this implies a further acceleration to 65.3% YoY. Given this, Q4 Datacenter revenue is likely to accelerate as well.

Gross Margin Expected to Reach the Mid-70% By Year-End

NVDA's margin profile continues to improve. Q3 non-GAAP gross margin came in at 74.6%, which topped the high end of 74% provided in Q2. Management previously guided that its non-GAAP gross margin will return to the mid-70s by the end of FY2026, which is confirmed in the Q4 outlook.

Moreover, non-GAAP operating margin is also recovering, showing better production efficiency after the full ramp-up of Blackwell chips. Based on the midpoints of Q4 guidance, we can calculate that the operating margin is expected to reach 67.3%. Overall, NVDA's growth and margin remain solid, which should support the current valuation.

$500 Billion Revenue for Blackwell and Rubin in 2026 and 2027

As Jensen indicated, Blackwell demand has been extraordinarily strong. GB300 revenue has already surpassed GB200, now representing two-thirds of Blackwell revenue. The next-gen Rubin platform is set to begin ramping up in 2H CY2026. According to the earnings call, Nvidia platform content per GW of AI datacenter will continue to improve each generation. For example, the company could generate around $20 to 25 billion of AI capex per GW of power from Hopper. $30 billion for Blackwell. Rubin is expected to be higher than that.

Let's say if OpenAI (OPENAI) were to build its 10 GW AI infrastructure with Rubin and we assume $35 billion of Nvidia platform content per GW, this would suggest that NVDA can capture $350 billion of AI spending (not its revenue) over the course of deployment.

During the earnings call, many analysts asked about the $500 billion revenue from Blackwell and Rubin in 2025 and 2026 that the company discussed at GTC. Management reiterated that they are on track to achieve this target. However, I believe it's challenging because it would imply another $350 billion in revenue in the next 14 months.

Now, NVDA expects the total AI capex among hyperscalers to reach $600 billion, nearly triple the $200 billion at the beginning of this year. This makes total sense to me. I recently wrote an earnings analysis on Meta Platforms (META), where I addressed that its FY2026 capex will be significantly higher even after an 81% YoY growth in FY2025.

Why I'm Still Cautious on NVDA's Earnings Consensus?

When everything looks strong on the surface, there can be cracks underneath. Although NVDA's valuation has already reflected the robust AI demand, the stock remains highly sensitive to any economic slowdown or fears of AI overspending. Management is unlikely to tell you about an ongoing demand slowdown until it has actually already happened, which means their forecasts will be unlikely to consider macro shocks that could disrupt near-term demand. In my opinion, the risk of a slowing U.S. economy is moving higher, and job data could deteriorate further in 2026.

Another concern is how quickly and aggressively the AI spending outlook has shifted. If upward revisions continue, does it mean that NVDA is pulling forward even more revenue growth from the future? These AI hyperscalers are front-loading their AI spending. We also don't know if these massive AI infrastructure buildouts will actually be profitable enough. Nevertheless, NVDA generates revenue as long as AI spending remains strong, regardless of whether this spending can eventually bring high ROIs to hyperscalers.

Conclusion

In summary, NVDA's Q3 FY2026 results were still solid, and the Q4 outlook suggests the AI hype will continue. However, solid earnings and robust AI demand do not address the concern around AI overspending, because NVDA makes money as long as there is growing demand for its GPUs. In addition, the big jump in the 2026 AI spending outlook also makes me cautious, especially since the market assumes no near-term economic slowdown. I believe NVDA's forward earnings consensus is priced for perfection, and the flip side of the "everything is sold out" story is that AI hyperscalers may be pulling forward demand to a level where it could quickly reverse. For that reason, I'm still neutral on NVDA.

data.title: Palantir: Nvidia's Earnings Changes Everything
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) before its Q3 FY25 earnings, I reiterated my Buy rating. I said that its partnership with Nvidia (NVDA) could provide the next leg higher in Palantir's stock, as management will likely guide revenue higher than consensus estimates from faster-than-expected enterprise AI adoption and deployment.

Palantir indeed had phenomenal Q3 earnings, where both revenue and earnings smashed expectations, with management substantially raising forward guidance, aided by explosive AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform) adoption as enterprises started to deploy agentic AI applications at scale.

Unfortunately, Palantir stock has been under pressure, losing 20% of its value from its peak, as renewed AI bubble concerns have ushered back into the market following Michael Burry's puts in both Nvidia and Palantir, along with tighter liquidity conditions and monetary policy uncertainty.

The way I see it, however, Nvidia's Q3 FY25 earnings yesterday should put a stop to the wavering sentiment in the AI landscape, as CEO Jensen Huang made it clear that we are still at an early stage in the AI buildout cycle.

With investor sentiment likely to inflect higher post Nvidia's earnings, I believe it will provide the much-needed boost that Palantir stock needs to start its next leg higher. As a result, I will continue to reiterate my Buy rating on the stock with a price target of $200 per share, representing an upside of 21% from current levels.

Nvidia Just Ended Michael Burry's "Short the Cycle" Narrative

The AI narrative has been under severe pressure since the start of November, as markets were held hostage to the 43-day U.S. government shutdown, ushering in growing liquidity concerns in the financial system and the monetary policy uncertainty. This was accompanied by the Big Short investor Michael Burry becoming bearish on both Nvidia and Palantir on potential AI bubble concerns, which has led to valuation contraction in several high-growth AI names in the ecosystem.

However, the results from Nvidia's Q3 earnings from yesterday should be sufficient to put a stop to the immediate bearishness in the AI landscape.

During the earnings call, Nvidia's Jensen Huang flat-out said that the AI bubble narrative is misplaced, especially as the world is undergoing three massive platform shifts/transitions all at once. The first transition includes the shift from CPU to GPU accelerated computing, with many legacy applications that are now moving to CUDA GPUs. The second platform shift is taking place at hyperscalers as GenAI reshapes their business models, translating into accelerated revenue growth and operating leverage. The third and final wave is that of agentic & physical AI that are capable of reasoning, planning, and using tools, marking the next frontier of computing.

For Nvidia, these are not just words. During the quarter, revenue beat guidance by over 3.3%, while management guided for Q4 revenues to grow 300 basis points higher than what analysts were estimating. Not only that, revenue next year should be up at least 54%, way above analyst estimates at 41%, with margins anchored at current levels.

All of this points to one thing.

We are still early in the AI buildout phase. This is exactly what Advanced Micro Devices' (AMD) CEO Lisa Su also pointed out not too long ago, calling it a 10-year supercycle.

What Does This Mean for Palantir?

Palantir reported its Q3 FY25 earnings at the start of the month. Going into earnings, management expected to accelerate its revenue for a ninth consecutive quarter, growing 50% YoY with an adjusted operating margin of 46%.

Well, the actual results were far more impressive than the guidance, with total revenues growing a whopping 63%, 1300 basis points higher than management's expectations, while operating margins came in 500 basis points higher than expected at 51%.

We can tie this to the acceleration in both Commercial and Government revenues, which grew 73% and 55% YoY, respectively, with particular strength in US Commercial revenue, which grew an eye-watering 121% YoY to $397M, contributing over a third of Palantir's total revenues.

I had often compared the position of Palantir's AIP in the agentic AI wave to how Nvidia's CUDA is positioned for the AI infrastructure layer. In fact, this is what I wrote in one of my earlier posts below.

"You see, Nvidia was at the center of the first wave of the AI Revolution, which is otherwise known as the training cycle. It was the case because Nvidia's CUDA is the monopoly operating layer for accelerated compute/intelligence.

In other words, once an enterprise builds a model in CUDA, it is optimized for Nvidia hardware and scalable only through Nvidia-compatible architecture. I am talking about an ecosystem lock-in with CUDA that ultimately positioned Nvidia to become the leader in the training cycle of the AI Revolution.

Now, as we enter the second wave of the AI Revolution, which is centered on organizations building applications with AI, I believe AIP is similarly positioned like Nvidia's CUDA was in the training phase.

What I mean is that as we shift from infrastructure/training to the application/inference phase of the AI Revolution, we need to solve for deployment and monetization.

This is where AIP's AI-native control layer embeds itself across the entire operating stack of a company, takes real-time data across systems, and turns it into actionable simulations across business cases."

On top of this, Nvidia and Palantir had also announced a strategic partnership at Nvidia's GTC conference last month, where it would see the integration of Nvidia's accelerated computing, CUDA-X libraries, and Nemotron open AI models into Palantir's Ontology framework to enable advanced data processing, automation, and context-aware AI agents across operating environments of retailers, healthcare providers, financial services, and the public sector.

This, I had argued in my previous post, is what could lead the next leg higher in Palantir's stock, as forward estimates could be revised much higher, especially as we continue to underestimate the amount of inference we need with enterprises deploying agentic AI applications at scale.

We are already seeing the above translating into an accelerating number of deals closed worth over $1M+, $5M+, and $10M+, while U.S. Commercial Total Contract Value has expanded a gargantuan 212% YoY to $843M during the quarter, driven both by the expansion at existing customers and the acquisition of new customers.

It doesn't look like the momentum is fading anytime soon.

For the full year FY25, management has now raised their revenue guidance by 6% from the previous quarter to $4.4B. This would represent a year-over-year revenue growth of 54%, compared to 28% in FY24. What is also important is Palantir will likely deliver revenue acceleration in Q4, marking 10 consecutive quarters of revenue acceleration, based on current consensus estimates. Meanwhile, when it comes to their profitability guidance, management has also raised their forward expectation for non-GAAP operating margin by roughly 300 basis points to 49%.

What AI Bubble?

This morning, I came across an X post where the author posted the chart below that shows that the S&P 500's P/E ratio has gone literally nowhere in 2025, despite a 13% YTD gain in the index levels. This means the rally is fully supported by earnings growth instead of multiple expansion.

Not typical of what you see in a "bubble" environment.

To that, you might say that Palantir should be treated differently, as it boasts a forward P/E ratio of 166, higher than any other enterprise software company in the universe.

Fair enough. I have got three points for you.

1. First, Palantir's forward P/E ratio has come down from its peak during the year at 250 to 166. In fact, at the end of last year, its forward P/E ratio stood at 158. This means its current forward P/E ratio of 166 is just 5% above where it was at the end of last year, while the stock is up over 118% during this period of time. Meanwhile, over the last 6 months, Palantir's forward earnings per share have been revised higher by 24.39%, as can be seen below.

2. The same is true for forward revenue growth estimates, which have been revised higher for FY25, FY26, and FY27 by 13.10%, 23.05%, and 27.73%, respectively, over the last 6 months.

3. Finally, Palantir scored 114% in its Rule of 40 score, which is unmatched by any other enterprise software company, as can be seen below. A big part of that has to do with its go-to-market strategy that is centered on AIP Bootcamps and AIPCon to unlock potential AI use cases for existing and new clients instead of hoarding salespeople.

Revisiting My Price Target

Taking the forward consensus estimates for revenue growth into account and assuming that Palantir would be able to anchor its gross margins at 82%, while normalizing the growth rate of its operating expenses from 20% in FY25 to 7% in the following years, we arrive at a total non-GAAP operating income of $5.2B by FY27.

This represents a margin expansion of roughly 1300 basis points to 62% from current projected levels of FY25, as it sees expanding operating leverage from deepening AIP adoption across its Commercial business across use cases, along with continued strength in its Government business.

With earnings projected to grow 50%+ at a compounded annual growth rate between FY25 and FY27, I believe it deserves a premium of at least 6x that of the average S&P 500, where companies grow their earnings at a 9% rate every year over a 10-year period.

This will translate to a P/E ratio of 115, or a price target of $200 per share, representing an upside of 21% from its current levels.

Note that my price target of $200 per share is slightly above the average Wall Street price target of $185 per share, while below the highest estimate of $255 per share.

Risks and Conclusions

Despite phenomenal Q3 earnings, along with a substantial raise in forward guidance, Palantir has not been able to stage a rally yet. Like I discussed earlier, this is tied to the general pessimism in the AI landscape since the start of the month, as investors have grown cautious of high-multiple "speculative" businesses with little to no earnings to show for it.

Fortunately, when it comes to Palantir, it has robust fundamentals to support its high valuation. Having said that, it is nonetheless subject to the general sentiment shifts in the AI landscape, which currently is one of pessimism.

The way I see it, we should see an inflection following Nvidia's Q3 earnings yesterday. Yet, until the S&P 500 can successfully close above its 20DMA levels, there remains the risk of potential downward pressure on the sector.

As a long-term investor in Palantir and many other AI names, I view the current market behavior more as a valuation reset than a crash. Especially when nothing has changed in the AI landscape on a fundamental level.

As a result, I will continue to reiterate my Buy rating on Palantir with a price target of $200 per share, representing an upside of 21% from its current levels.

data.title: WPAY: A ~55% Weekly Return Comes With 100% Risk
data.description:
Over the past couple of years, but really I would say quarters, there has been an immense growth in different kinds of yield-focused ETFs. The lion's share of that universe is definitely attributable to the covered call ETF vehicles, which, on average, offer a yield between 10% and 15% that comes with a compelling downside protection mechanism.

For example, we have such covered call ETFs as the NEOS Nasdaq 100 High Income ETF (QQQI) and the NEOS S&P 500(R) High Income ETF (SPYI) that have gained a lot of traction in the market. Many investors have also recognized the innovation that is associated with dynamic asset management, which is something that strongly differentiates these new funds from the old covered call industry giants - the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ).

On top of that, the innovation has also extended to the choice of underlying assets that the covered call ETFs choose in order to capture option premiums. My personal favorites are the NEOS Real Estate High Income ETF (IYRI) and the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI), which provide access to the covered call ETF factor that is not in any shape or form linked to the overvalued AI sector through the S&P 500 (SPY) and/or the Nasdaq-100 (QQQ) exposures.

In this case, I have identified the Roundhill WeeklyPay Universe ETF (WPAY) - an ETF managed by Roundhill, which has developed some really compelling covered call ETFs.

Without going into the details, it is clear that WPAY is meant for high-yield-focused investors. If we annualized the average value of two most recent distributions, we would arrive at a yield of ~55%. Plus, and this could be a real differentiator for many investors, the yield we get from WPAY comes in the form of weekly payments.

On the surface, it seems that WPAY is something similar to one of the high-yield YMAX covered call ETFs or Roundhill's own - Roundhill Innovation-100 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (QDTE), which currently produces a dividend yield of almost 40%.

However, by peeling back the onion a bit, we will understand that the income is accommodated through a completely different (covered call unrelated) mechanism. Let me explain.

Thesis

There are two major components that we have to understand.

First, WPAY is a fund-of-fund type of instrument.

It means that WPAY does not invest directly in some pure-play assets, but instead it simply channels capital towards a selected universe of individual Roundhill ETFs. These underlying ETFs are established as single-stock ETFs - i.e., each ETF invests in only one particular stock.

The portfolio consists of 15 underlying ETFs, which are structured on an equal-weight basis. If we looked at the current picture, we would notice some deviations from the equal-weight target values - which is fine given that the values do not remain static. To combat large and prolonged deviation, WPAY applies a monthly rebalancing process to bring the split back to the 'equilibrium.

Second, and this is where the substance lies, WPAY, at its core, is not a covered call ETF. It is a leveraged ETF, which takes a 120% long position in the selected/individual stock holdings through its underlying (15 selected) ETFs. Before I go into the details of what this implies for investors, I encourage you to take a look at the SEC disclosure (warning) about the potential consequences of investing in leveraged ETFs like WPAY.

Technically, WPAY does not use leverage. As stated in the first point, the only thing that WPAY does is go long on the 15 ETFs and rebalance them on a monthly basis (and for this service it charges a net fee of 0.99% of AuM). However, it is the underlying ETF universe that has chosen to employ leverage.

For example, we could take the three largest ETFs (which have become the largest due to positive deviation from the equal weight baseline): Roundhill GOOGL WeeklyPay ETF (GOOW), Roundhill AAPL WeeklyPay ETF (AAPW) and Roundhill BRKB WeeklyPay ETF (BRKW). These three and the remaining ones create value by doing the following:

  • Opening a long position in a specific stock.
  • Entering into a total return swap on a 120% exposure basis - paying fixed for the implied leverage part and receiving floating (i.e., total returns) from the realized performance of the chosen stock.
  • Distributing all of the realized gains in the form of dividends.

In a nutshell, the essence boils down to borrowing money to buy more stock than what you have invested by putting down your equity (saved capital).

Let's take a look at how AAPW has performed relative to Apple (AAPL) on a YTD basis:

We can see that in the case of downside, AAPW falls a bit more than pure-play common stock - AAPL. When AAPL recovers, AAPW tends to rebound a bit faster, but only until the next correction takes place, which throws AAPW back to square one.

So, the only scenario in which WPAY and its underlying ETFs could outperform is if the ETF-chosen stocks surge higher and do so in a relatively uninterrupted manner.

Conversely, if the market becomes very volatile or goes down, it is almost certain that WPAY will underperform.

One might wonder here what happens if the market trades sideways - i.e., remains flat. Well, in such a case, WPAY should also likely underperform because of the cost of leverage, which is associated with total return swap positions. On top of that, we have to remember the operational/holding costs of the ETF, which can further accelerate the value erosion process. Also, think of it: if the asset remains completely flat, for pure-play equity holders the returns would be around 0%. For WPAY investors, the longer the market remains flat or, even worse, is on a gradual decline path, the larger the underperformance gap becomes because of the inherent cost of leverage and magnified loss mechanism.

Finally, some could indeed carry a bullish view on the market, and thus be willing to express that view through 1.2x leverage. Then the question becomes what really sits inside of WPAY.

In the table below we can see the top current positions of WPAY, which are clearly biased towards, in my view, the overvalued AI segment. In other words, there are many overlaps with the QQQ index, which is the last thing, where I would like to apply 1.2x leverage:

The bottom line

In my opinion, investors have to be very, very careful and think twice before deploying their capital in WPAY. At the end of the day, the total returns and the long-term dividend income boil down to how well the AI-driven names perform from here. So, with slight exceptions, it is a 1.2x leveraged bet on the Nasdaq-100 names, which have started to show some first signs of weakness (while still trading close to record highs).

The historical weekly dividend distribution chart captures the story nicely:

Here, we can already see the consequences of the recently struggling QQQ and how immediate (material) the distribution reductions are. And we have not even yet experienced a steep correction to the downside.

data.title: Voya Small Cap Growth Strategy Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

  • Equity markets rebounded in the third quarter of 2025, recovering from April's tariff-driven volatility and ending well above mid-year levels. Growth outperformed value, led by strong gains in technology and communication services, while consumer staples lagged, remaining the sole sector detractor, driven by early-quarter weakness. Easing inflation, selective rate cuts, and increased demand for safe-haven assets shaped cross-asset dynamics. Higher beta and deep value also participated meaningfully in the rally.
  • For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, the Fund outperformed the Russell 2000 Growth Index (the Index) on a net asset value (NAV) basis, due to stock selection. Positive stock selection in the technology, financials, and consumer staples sectors were the leading contributors to outperformance. An overall negative allocation effect, specifically cash, modestly detracted from performance for the period.
  • As we enter the final stretch of the year, investors face persistent geopolitical tensions, policy uncertainty, and inflation risks. Market leadership is expanding beyond mega-cap stocks, creating opportunities in defensive and cyclical sectors. We aim to remain nimble amid ongoing macroeconomic and market uncertainty.

Portfolio review

For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, the Fund outperformed the Index on a NAV basis, due to stock selection. Positive stock selection in the technology, financials, and consumer staples sectors were the leading contributors to outperformance. An overall negative allocation effect, specifically cash, modestly detracted from performance for the period.

Top individual contributors included Tower Semiconductor Ltd. (TSEM), Lumentum Holdings, Inc. (LITE), and Applied Digital Corp. (APLD)

Tower Semiconductor Ltd. (TSEM) a specialty manufacturer of customized analog semiconductors used in diverse industries including automotive, medical, consumer, and industrial markets. Tower Semiconductor reported 2Q25 revenue of $372.1 million, a 6% year-over-year increase, exceeding estimates. TSEM's exposure to the continuing artificial intelligence infrastructure buildout, combined with a solid balance sheet and history of developing profitable strategic partnerships makes the stock a solid holding with a meaningful weight in our portfolio, although we are mindful of the valuation expansion.

Lumentum Holdings, Inc. (LITE), a provider of optical and photonic products utilized in cloud, networking and industrial applications, saw its stock price rise over 70% during the third quarter and was on our list of top-performers yet again. LITE's Cloud Light data center unit, which supports technologies critical for AI and cloud infrastructure, continued to grow and LITE exceeded company guidance for the fiscal fourth quarter (June 2025) and gave initial 2026 guidance that exceeded consensus estimates. Although we continue to hold the stock and believe LITE is well positioned for continued near and mid-term growth from solid demand, we have trimmed the position given the substantial valuation expansion and higher expectations.

Applied Digital Corp. (APLD), a company that engages in the development and operation of data centers which provide computing power to both crypto mining, High Performance Computing (HPC) and AI customers, was a top contributor for the third quarter. After adding to the position in early July, the stock more than doubled as expectations of another HPC agreement by the end of the year for its 280MW Harwood, ND site seemed imminent. As APDL continues to reduce build time and pull from an increasingly knowledgeable labor force, we believe the company is well positioned to build additional marketable sites and expand their pipeline. We continue to hold our position.

Top individual detractors to performance included Bloom Energy Corp. (BE), Kura Sushi USA, Inc. (KRUS), and CAVA Group, Inc. (CAVA)

Bloom Energy Corp. (BE) manufactures and installs solid oxide fuel-cell based power generation platforms which converts standard low-pressure natural gas or biogas into electricity. The stock represented a 1.07% weighting within the Russell 2000 Growth benchmark at the end of the quarter and the stock continued to re-rate up based on strong demand and positioning within the AI power generation segment. We bought the stock during the quarter however our relative underweight (0.76% versus 1.07% at the end of the quarter) detracted from performance during the quarter. We continue to hold the name and recognize the near-term strong demand trends in power generation benefitting BE.

Kura Sushi USA, Inc. (KRUS), an operator of sushi restaurants emphasizing technology to create a unique customer experience, underperformed for the quarter. Negative sentiment across the restaurant sector coupled with tariff concerns and comparable sales headwinds impacted KRUS stock performance during the quarter. We continue to believe unit development will remain Kura's most meaningful driver of revenue growth, with annual expansion in excess of 20% square footage growth. However, given the current overall consumer weakness, investor patience has waned. At quarter end we held our position and are monitoring accordingly.

CAVA Group, Inc. (CAVA) an operator of Mediterranean inspired fast-casual restaurants and producer of select dips, spreads, and dressings, was a detractor. Following disappointing 2Q25 results that saw comparable sales and revenue miss analyst estimates, CAVA cut its full year same-store sales growth forecast. This provided insight into potential 3Q25 softness and investors sold the stock as a result. We trimmed our position during the quarter and continue to hold the name on a short leash.

Current strategy and outlook

Small cap stocks have rallied with the Russell 2000 Growth posting a gain of over 43% since the April 8, 2025 lows, through the end of the third quarter. Even in the face of continued tariff threats, investors and company managements appear to have gained comfort with several tariff deals now in place, although there is still an uncertain outcome with China. In the face of mixed inflationary data that saw a brief reacceleration of headline Consumer Price Index (CPI) and core CPI holding steady in August, several economic reports during the quarter were indicative of a slowing economy. None was more telling than the latest jobs report showing fewer openings and jobs added. This forced the U.S. Federal Reserve to consider their dual mandate and ultimately led to a 25 basis points reduction in the fed funds rate in mid-September, the first since the start of the easing cycle last December.

As it stands today, we believe small caps are poised to continue their move upwards. A Fed easing cycle typically leads to an acceleration in earnings for small caps. Projected earnings growth for small caps is expected to surpass large caps in 4Q25, 2026 and 2027. This favorable backdrop coupled with substantial discounted valuations relative to large caps have ignited a strong small cap rally over the last several months.

We continue to focus on individual company fundamental factors and seek to identify companies that can grow revenue, expand margins and produce solid cash flow and earnings, all while seeking sustainable valuations. The momentum factor resulting from the ongoing AI theme, combined with Fed easing, has elevated many stock prices and valuations. In this environment, we remain cognizant of the risk versus reward balance within the portfolio and at the individual company level. However, we are starting to see the early signs of a favorable backdrop for some of our more cyclical oriented holdings that can provide substantial “earnings torque” and are currently trading within an attractive valuation bandwidth.

As mentioned last quarter, we continue to witness unprecedented dynamics within our Russell 2000 Growth benchmark that we have not seen in the last 30 years of managing small cap growth.

Holdings detail

Companies mentioned in this report—percentage of portfolio investments, as of 09/30/25: Tower Semiconductor Ltd. 2.65%, Lumentum Holdings, Inc. 1.19%, Applied Digital Corp. 1.11%, Bloom Energy Corp. 0.89%, Kura Sushi USA, Inc. 0.74%, and CAVA Group, Inc. 0.33%; 0% indicates that the security is no longer in the Fund. Portfolio holdings are subject to daily change.

Voya Small Cap Growth Fund

Focusing on High-Quality Companies with Sustainable Growth Trends

Michael Coyne, CFAPortfolio Manager

Mitchell Brivic, CFAPortfolio Manager

Joel RubensteinPortfolio Manager

Strategy overview

Actively managed small cap growth strategy driven by bottom-up fundamental research seeking stocks with superior revenue and earnings potential and sustainable valuations.

The Russell 2000 Growth Index is an unmanaged index that measures the performance of smaller U.S. companies with greater-than-average growth orientation. It is a small-cap stock market index that makes up the smallest 2,000 stocks in the Russell 3000 Index. Index returns do not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

Investors should consult the Fund's Prospectus and Statement of Additional Information for a more detailed discussion of the Fund's risks.

The strategy employs a quantitative model to execute the strategy. Data imprecision, software or other technology malfunctions, programming inaccuracies and similar circumstances may impair the performance of these systems, which may negatively affect performance. Furthermore, there can be no assurance that the quantitative models used in managing the strategy will perform as anticipated or enable the strategy to achieve its objective.

An annuity does not provide any additional tax deferral benefit, as tax deferral is provided by the plan. Annuities may be subject to additional fees and expenses to which other tax-qualified funding vehicles may not be subject. However, an annuity does provide other features and benefits, such as lifetime income payments and death benefits, which may be valuable to you.
